one_line_takeaway: SMTA + Burn-After-Use gives strong (92%) empirical isolation against cross-tenant prompt/infrastructure attacks and moderate (76.75% average) protection against post-session data persistence, but the weakest BAU failure mode -- OS-level screen capture (~50% exposure) -- is a modeled estimate outside the mechanism's control, not something the design actually fixes.
key_finding: SMTA achieves a 92% combined defense success rate against 55 infrastructure-level cross-tenant attack iterations (near-total resistance to prompt-based leakage, but VCLR approx 6% and TLLR = 10% show residual risk from credential misconfiguration and shared logging/observability pipelines). BAU achieves an average 76.75% success rate across 72 iterations spanning four independent failure classes; the weakest link by far is Test Case 3 (OS-level screen/frame capture, IFER approx 50%), which the authors explicitly note is outside the BAU mechanism's control since it governs application-managed caches/files, not OS compositors -- meaning even a perfectly correct BAU implementation cannot close this gap.
core_contribution: The paper proposes two combined mechanisms for enterprise LLM deployments: (1) a Secure Multi-Tenant Architecture (SMTA) that maps organizational departments to isolated logical tenants (each with its own LLM instance/container, dedicated memory space, and vector store), enforced via combined RBAC/ABAC access control and a mnemonic-based (BIP-39, 12-word/132-bit entropy) decentralized authentication scheme in place of centralized username-password stores; and (2) a Burn-After-Use (BAU) mechanism that makes conversational context, document-derived tokens, and embeddings ephemeral -- destroyed at session timeout or user termination via client-side local deletion plus a stateless server-side inference policy -- to prevent post-session inference, prompt-extraction, and session-replay attacks. The authors evaluate both mechanisms empirically using gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2, and reference an existing open-source client, ToutCas (github.com/VictorZhang2014/toutcas), as the reference implementation of BAU whose deletion code paths (e.g., burnChatConversation() in ./lib/src/views/home_view.dart) are directly tested.
threat_model: Two threat surfaces are evaluated. (A) Cross-tenant leakage: an attacker operating within one tenant (e.g., HR, Finance, R&D) issues adversarial prompts attempting to extract another tenant's confidential knowledge base, plus infrastructure-level attacks -- vector-database credential compromise (password guessing / credential leakage) and shared-logging-pipeline exposure (log entries visible to the wrong tenant). (B) Post-session/burn persistence: after a BAU 'burn' event, an attacker probes the system to see whether previously submitted sensitive data (KV-cache, session embeddings, conversation history, logs, transient memory) can still be reconstructed, paraphrased, or influences outputs. Failure modes tested for BAU specifically include: local cache deletion failure on app crash, remote cache persistence under network failure, OS-level screen/frame capture (outside the app's control), and race conditions on the burn timer under OS scheduling interference (App Nap, Suspend, low-power mode, blocking I/O).
limitations: Self-acknowledged by the authors: (1) SMTA's isolation boundaries are software-configured, so per-tenant hardware-level cost is not reduced; (2) BAU's guarantees depend on the correctness of runtime cache-clearing code, requiring continuous verification across different LLM/client stacks; (3) introducing a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline may create additional persistence risk beyond the session boundary that BAU does not currently address. Additional issues observable from the text itself: the Image Frame Exposure Rate (IFER = 50%) is explicitly a modeled/assumed value ('We model the exposure as approximately 50%'), not an empirically measured quantity, unlike the other three BAU metrics -- this weakens the 76.75% average, one of whose four inputs is not empirical. The cross-tenant CTLR result is reported only qualitatively ('CTLR approx 0', 'no attack prompt succeeded') without a numeric denominator/count of adversarial prompts tested, despite CTLR being formally defined with equations. The EDM (Exact Decision Match) metric is formally defined but no numeric result is reported for it. Sample sizes for individual sub-tests are small (10-35 trials per test), limiting statistical confidence in the reported rates. Table 3 in the PDF lists the BTPR row's success rate as '0.80%', which is inconsistent with the in-text derivation (Success Rate = 1 - BTPR approx 80%) and with the stated average of 76.75% (which only reconciles if the BTPR success rate is 80%, not 0.80%) -- an apparent typo in the paper's own table.

datasets_used: Simulated three-tenant enterprise scenario with department-specific confidential documents (Tenant H/HR employee salary records, Tenant F/Finance budget plans and forecasts, Tenant R/R&D proprietary algorithm design notes) -- author-constructed, not a public benchmark dataset
quantitative_results: Overall cross-tenant infrastructure-level defense success rate: 92% across 55 attack iterations (Appendix A)
Burn Failure Rate (BFR): ≈23.25% across 72 BAU test iterations (Appendix B)
Image Frame Exposure Rate (IFER): ≈50% (success rate 50%; modeled/assumed, not empirically measured)
Average BAU success rate across four case studies: 76.75%
Mnemonic authentication entropy: 132 bits for a 12-word BIP-39 phrase from a 2048-word dictionary
baselines_compared: No algorithmic/model baselines are compared quantitatively; the paper instead contrasts SMTA/BAU qualitatively against conventional enterprise controls (ACLs, encryption, network segmentation) and against public LLM access modes (public conversational interfaces, API invocation) via a qualitative comparison table (Table 1) covering data retention policy, caching type, leakage risk, and deployment cost, not a benchmarked security baseline.

Three LLMs used as the deployed inference engines under test: gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 (used as the systems under evaluation, not compared against each other as baselines)
